dialogos.texto_en: The Ocean Culture Museum over there apparently has\na Nautical Zone, Oceania Zone, and a Cultural Exchange\nZone, where you can experience sea related history.
There's also a planetarium, I think.
Yeah, there is one. By the way,\nthe starry sky in Okinawa should be quite different\nfrom what you usually see.
I know that.\nThis place is much further south than Honshu.
Speaking of which...\nWhen you think of Okinawa, you'd think of the Southern Cross, \nright?
Oh! You know your stuff.\nYeah, around here, it should be barely visible\nright near the horizon!
Huh? Barely visible?
Yeah, the stars in the Southern Cross are\nrepresentative stars of the southern hemisphere.\nTo see it properly, you have to go further south.
Ok, I get it.\nI thought you could normally see it in Okinawa.
If you go further south, we're no longer in Japan.\nLike in Hawaii?
Yeah, in Hawaii, you can definitely see it.\nI think you can also see it\non some southern islands in Japan...
Really?\nWait a minute, let me look that up.
Ah, using your smartphone.\nReally comes in handy in times like this.
Let's see, there's a remote island\nin the southernmost part of Okinawa\ncalled Hateruma Island, where you can see it.
Oh, I see.
Places to see the cross are really limited in Japan.\nGoing to the southernmost inhabited island\nis quite a high hurdle.
Oh, by the way, there's also a constellation\ncalled the False Cross\nthat's often mistaken for the Southern Cross.
The False Cross? What a name.
There's something romantic about remote islands and the \nsouthernmost tip!
Yes, I can see why people love it.\nIt has an adventurous feel to it.
I heard there are tours to see the Southern Cross\non Hateruma Island,\nand there is a tower for stargazing too.
Thought so, since the Southern Cross is a rare sight,\nthe environment for observation must be well prepared.
We can't go this time, but someday,\nit might be nice to go see the Southern Cross.
What about you, Futaro?
With you, you mean?
Of course.\nLet's go chase the romantic dream together.
Well, I don't think that's a bad idea at all.
Oh, by the way, there's also a constellation\ncalled the False Cross\nthat's often mistaken for the Southern Cross.
The False Cross? What a name.
Stargazing would be nice.\nI mean, going anywhere with you would be fun.
Huh...?
Ah, well, I don't really know how you feel,\nbut at least I feel that way.
I-I feel the same!\nObviously!
However, Futaro, you said it so suddenly.\nDon't...surprise me like that.
Sorry.\nTo be honest, I think I said something embarrassing\nin the heat of the moment.
Hehe!\nWell, then we're even.
Oh, by the way, there's also a constellation\ncalled the False Cross\nthat's often mistaken for the Southern Cross.
The False Cross? What a name.
The original Southern Cross is small\nand hard to distinguish. So many people mistake other\nstars in a cross shape for the Southern Cross.
And that's why they call it the False Cross.
My, my.\nIt's a terrible thing to mistake something,\nand THEN call it a fake.
Are you upset about something?
I can understand how the accused stars feel.\nAs quintuplets,\nwe often get mistaken for each other.
It can't be helped,\nbut sometimes people say unreasonable things.
(Ahh...\n Quintuplets have their own struggles, huh.)
They shouldn't call it the False Cross.\nWhy not give it a name instead?
The False Cross connects stars from\nother constellations, so it might be difficult\nto establish another name for it.
